our_argument_summary: The executive summary frames the review's verdict breakdown (0 Actual Wins, 9 Refuted by Data, 12 Self-Contradicted, 23 Misleading, 16 Std Model Explains, 3 Not Demonstrated, 4 Unfalsifiable) and establishes methodological principles. The review claims no dome claim produces results the globe model cannot explain. It emphasizes internal consistency checks and fairness. The Evaluation Guide section articulates five principles of the review and explains why a 'shared prediction' between two models is not evidence for either.

holes_found.description: The opening scorecard claims '0 Actual Wins' with the subtitle 'Not one claim produces a result that the globe model can't explain and the dome uniquely can.' This is logically sound, but it is IMPOSSIBLY HARD to defend if dome defenders exploit the word 'uniquely.' A dome defender can argue: 'You admit the dome predicts these observations. You admit the globe also predicts them. But you haven't shown a measurement that CONTRADICTS the dome while matching the globe. Your 0 Actual Wins is a false dichotomy — the dome is not falsified if it matches all the same data as the globe, it's just less parsimonious. By your own Evaluation Guide logic, neither model is "proven" by shared predictions, but that doesn't mean the dome produces zero correct predictions — it produces the same predictions as the globe, just with more complexity.' This is a loophole in the framing: we conflate 'zero wins' (dome-specific predictive power) with 'all predictions are wrong,' when in fact most dome predictions are correct — they're just not unique.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the language in the scorecard. Change '0 Actual Wins' to '0 Dome-Exclusive Wins' or '0 Unique Predictive Successes.' The current phrasing invites the rebuttal 'the dome does predict these correctly, just not alone.' Also, add a sentence in the Evaluation Guide (Principle 3) explicitly stating: 'A prediction that both models make equally accurately is evidence for neither model's exclusive correctness, and counts as zero discriminating wins. This is standard in comparative model selection.' This preempts the 'but we got the data right' response.

holes_found.description: The Evaluation Guide's principle on 'Does the prediction distinguish this model?' uses the medicine analogy (Theory A: cell biology, Theory B: spiritual energy both predict bleeding stops). This is pedagogically effective, but it assumes a dome defender accepts the premise. A sophisticated dome defender will counter: 'Your analogy proves too much. The globe model also uses cellular metaphors and invokes gravity, dark matter, convection, etc. — unobservable mechanisms just like our aether. By your standard, ALL models that fit data equally are indistinguishable. Yet you default to globe-side parsimony without justifying why parsimony beats data fit.' The analogy doesn't actually address why the globe model's additional assumptions (curved space-time, dark matter, dark energy, three-body problem complexity) are acceptable while the dome's additional assumptions (aether, flat disc, local sun) are not.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace or augment the medicine analogy with a head-to-head comparison: 'Model A (globe) requires 8 core parameters plus dark matter, dark energy, gravitational wave theory, and special relativity to explain {list 5 key observations}. Model B (dome) requires 6 core parameters plus aether and local sun geometry to explain the same observations. Both fit the data. Why prefer A over B?' Then answer: 'Because Model A's additional parameters have independent confirmation (LIGO, Planck, gravitational lensing). Model B's additional parameters (aether density, local sun mechanics) do not. THIS is why parsimony matters — when two models tie on data fit, the one with independently-confirmed auxiliary hypotheses wins.' The current Evaluation Guide implies the dome loses on principle; it should argue the dome loses on evidence.

holes_found.description: The scorecard breakdown lists 67 total WINs but never explains why there are 67 instead of 39 (the dome's own claim in raw-text/02-wins.txt). The raw text shows 'Version 50.6, 2026-03-12, 39 confirmed, 0 falsified.' The review appears to have expanded this set. A dome defender will immediately ask: 'You're reviewing their V51.0 claims. They claim 39 WINs. Why does your scorecard show 67?' The answer (the review analyzed sub-components and derived claims not explicitly counted by the dome author) is fair, but it's not explained in the overview. This creates a perception that we're inflating the dataset.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a footnote to the main scorecard: 'The dome model registers 39 primary WINs in V50.6. V51.0 adds new geomagnetic claims and refines earlier ones. This review analyzes 67 claims derived from V51.0's published predictions page and archival documents, grouping related sub-predictions (e.g., tidal constituents M2, S2, K1, O1) as individual WINs. See Part 2.1 for the complete table mapping our 67 WINs to the dome's registration numbers.' This transparency prevents the objection 'you moved the goalposts.'

holes_found.description: Principle 5 states: 'Unfalsifiable claims are identified, not ridiculed.' But the overview and scorecard conspicuously display 4 WINs as 'Unfalsifiable' (a gray, neutral color) without emphasizing this is a methodological classification, not a dismissal. A dome defender will argue: 'You're burying the fact that several of our claims are theological — which we're transparent about. You could have said 'theological claims lie outside the scope of empirical science, but 64 of our remaining claims are empirical and fit the data.' Instead you lump them as 'unfalsifiable' (gray), suggesting they're defective. That's not rigor, it's rhetorical downgrading.' The tone is fair, but the presentation needs to acknowledge that unfalsifiable ≠ false, just ≠ testable.
holes_found.recommendation: After the scorecard, add a brief sentence: 'Four WINs are theological in nature (e.g., biblical literalism, cosmological eschatology) and lie outside the scope of empirical science. We classify these separately to preserve clarity about what we can and cannot test, not to dismiss them as invalid perspectives. The remaining 63 claims are empirical and are evaluated on evidence.' This reframes unfalsifiable from 'defective' to 'out-of-scope' — methodologically honest without rhetorical skew.

holes_found.description: The Evaluation Guide says 'We do not dismiss the dome model simply because it disagrees with established science.' This is fair. But the summary verdict breakdown lists 16 'Std Model Explains' verdicts — a category that, by definition, concedes the observation is real and standard physics works. A dome defender will ask: 'So you admit 16 of our predictions are correct (matching your own data). Why is that not progress? You're saying the globe model explains these 16 observations — but those are our predictions too. The globe did not predict them first; we did.' This conflates 'the observation is explained by standard physics' with 'the dome model deserves no credit for predicting it.' If the dome predicted the Schumann frequency, magnetic pole drift, or SAA expansion before they were measured, claiming 'standard physics already explained it' is true but misses the predictive success.
holes_found.recommendation: In the 'Std Model Explains' section (Part 2.4 of the overview prose), explicitly distinguish post-hoc fits from genuine predictions: 'Some WINs cite observations that were already established when the dome model was published. Standard physics explained these before the dome model existed. Other WINs (like SAA exponential separation) cite measurements made after the dome claimed them, which could be genuine prospective predictions. Part 3.5 separates these. The 'Std Model Explains' verdict applies to both retroactive and prospective observations, because we ask: does the dome model add quantitative power beyond standard physics? In all 16 cases reviewed, the answer is no — standard physics provides equally precise predictions with simpler assumptions.' This acknowledges the dome's predictive claim while explaining why it doesn't change the verdict.

holes_found.description: The overview claims verdict tallies are 'computed at build time' and emphasize that counts are derived from data, not hardcoded. This is excellent practice. However, the summary prose doesn't actually cite those computed numbers — it uses template variables like ${counts.total}. A reader of the final HTML will see the numbers, but anyone reviewing this source file will see placeholders. If the generated HTML is what we're evaluating, the numbers are correct; if we're claiming the build pipeline is correct, we should show the derivation explicitly in this review (which I did separately).
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed — this is a non-issue for the final review. The HTML rendering is correct. Just note for clarity: the scorecard numbers (67 total, 9 refuted, 12 self-contradicted, etc.) are all computed from wins.json at build time and should match the counts I verified above. They do.

holes_found.description: The opening scorecard is visually striking: '0 Actual Wins' in a prominent card. This is good narrative emphasis, but it could be read as strawmanning. The dome claims 39-67 observations consistent with their model. We claim 0 that are uniquely explained by the dome. A dome defender's response: 'Your scorecard is a false binary: either the dome is the only model explaining Earth, or it has zero wins. What about 'partially explains', 'explains as well as alternatives', or 'explains with fewer auxiliary hypotheses'? Most models in science fit data without being the sole explanation. You've set the bar impossibly high.' We haven't — the bar is 'unique predictive power' — but the presentation looks like we have.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a subtitle to the '0 Actual Wins' card: '(dome-exclusive predictive power beyond standard physics)' or '(claims uniquely explained by dome geometry)'. This clarifies the threshold without softening it. Readers will understand we mean unique power, not any success at all.

stronger_arguments.argument: Computational Specificity > Narrative Fit: The dome model derives specific numbers (H(r) exponential, B(r) dipole formula, κ = 1.67 nT/μGal). Standard physics also derives specific numbers (Earth's 6,371 km radius, 41 mA/m^2 magnetic dipole moment, 9.8 m/s^2 gravity). When both models fit data equally well, the one whose auxiliary assumptions are independently confirmed (satellite orbits, LIGO detections, gravitational lensing) is rationally preferred. The dome's auxiliary assumptions (aether density, local sun at 5,733 km, firmament at 9,086 km) have zero independent confirmation. This is not about parsimony preference — it's about the burden of proof: new mechanisms require new evidence.
stronger_arguments.rationale: This argument is stronger than the general parsimony principle because it shifts the burden of proof. The dome is not wrong because it's complex; it's less credible because its complexity is unexplained by independent evidence. The current review hints at this but doesn't state it directly.

stronger_arguments.argument: Self-Contradiction Beats Refutation: When the dome's own geometry (H(r) = 8537×exp(−r/8619), B(r) two-pole formula) predicts a value that contradicts what the author claims, that's stronger than external refutation. It shows the model eats itself — no external measurement is needed to falsify it. The dome's Schumann cavity height (9,572 km from the Schumann-derived formula) exceeds the dome's own firmament ceiling (8,537 km) by 12%. This is not a measurement disagreement; it's an internal contradiction in the published parameters. The current review makes this point well (WIN-002, WIN-029) but could emphasize it more in the overview: internal contradictions should carry more weight than external refutations, because they require only the model's own statements, not appeals to external authority.
stronger_arguments.rationale: A dome defender will trust the dome's parameters over external measurements. Self-contradiction is harder to dismiss because it uses the dome's own words against itself. The overview doesn't emphasize this asymmetry.

stronger_arguments.argument: Hardcoded Validation > Hardcoded Claims: The dome claims 95.2% accuracy. The review found this is a hardcoded HTML string with no computation. This is damaging, but the stronger point is: what else is hardcoded? The code analysis tags show 20/31 reviewed WINs use hardcoded pred=obs checks in monitor.py. That means the model is not self-correcting — if a prediction fails, the code doesn't flag it, it just checks if pred==obs with the values hardcoded. This is not falsifiable monitoring; it's tautology automation. The overview mentions this (Part 4.6) but could emphasize in the executive summary: 'The model claims zero falsifications. The code ensures zero falsifications by hardcoding them. This is not because the model is immune to error — it's because the monitoring system is not actually testing anything.'
stronger_arguments.rationale: This argument cuts deeper than claiming 'the accuracy is hardcoded.' It shows the entire validation infrastructure is compromised. Any model can achieve 100% accuracy if it hardcodes the answers.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model does produce specific, internally-consistent mathematical structures. The H(r) exponential cavity, the B(r) two-pole dipole, the 1.67 nT/μGal coupling constant, and the claim that many geomagnetic phenomena (SAA, pole drift, tidal patterns) emerge from a unified toroidal geometry is not trivially false. If the aetheric medium existed with the claimed density and circulation pattern, some of the predictions would follow. The model is falsifiable in principle — we can check if the derived values match observations. The problem is not that the model is unfalsifiable, but that it is falsified repeatedly by its own parameters (Schumann contradiction, gravity at rim, solar diameter) and that its predictive successes are post-hoc fits to known data, not prospective predictions.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being internally self-consistent in a hypothetical cavity does not make the cavity real. The aetheric medium is not independently confirmed. The geometric parameters (disc radius, firmament height, sun altitude) are calibrated to fit known data, not derived from first principles. Even if the mathematics is correct, the model provides no mechanism to distinguish it from the globe model when both fit the same observations. And critically, the model contradicts itself on core claims (Schumann frequency, gravity distribution, solar angular size) — suggesting the internal consistency is only superficial, maintained by ignoring the model's own equations when they produce wrong answers.

sections_covered: How to Evaluate Scientific Claims (5 questions)

deeper_analysis:
The executive summary is well-structured and defensible, but vulnerable on three fronts:

1. **The 0 Wins framing is sound but can be reinterpreted.** A dome defender will say 'You admit we predict these observations correctly. You just say the globe does too. But that's not zero wins — that's a tie on data fit, which is not a loss.' The current summary conflates 'unique predictive power' with 'no correct predictions,' and the scorecard doesn't clarify the distinction until the Evaluation Guide. The fix is a clarifying subtitle on the scorecard.

2. **The Evaluation Guide is fair but doesn't justify the globe-side default.** When two models fit data equally, we prefer the simpler one (standard physics). But simplicity is a methodological preference, not a logical proof. A sophisticated dome defender will ask: 'Why is parsimony more important than data fit?' The answer is: 'Because the simpler model's auxiliary assumptions (curved spacetime, gravitational waves) are independently confirmed by non-model experiments. The dome's auxiliary assumptions (aether, local sun) are not.' This argument is stronger and more specific than the current appeal to parsimony, but it's not made explicitly in the overview.

3. **The post-hoc vs. prospective distinction is mentioned but not emphasized.** Several dome claims (like SAA exponential separation) were published before the measurements. If these are genuine prospective predictions, they deserve credit for predictive success, even if standard physics also explains them. The current overview doesn't emphasize this is a separate question from 'does it prove the dome is real?' This is a hole we could exploit to strengthen fairness and preempt the 'you're not giving us credit' objection.

4. **The Unfalsifiable category could be misread as dismissal.** Theological claims are not false, just untestable. The overview handles this well, but the visual presentation (gray color, small count) could give the impression we're relegating them to 'defective' rather than 'out-of-scope.' A sentence clarifying this distinction would improve perceived fairness.

Overall assessment: The executive summary is intellectually rigorous and the verdict breakdown is accurate (all 67 WINs tallied correctly, all categories applied consistently). The main vulnerabilities are in framing and emphasis, not in the actual verdicts. A dome defender will focus on reinterpreting 'zero wins' as 'no unique power' and challenging the default to standard physics. These are not fatal flaws in the summary, but they are predictable attacks that should be preempted in the prose.

Dome's own claim: 39 WINs in V50.6 (verified from raw-text/02-wins.txt, line 'Version 50.6, 2026-03-12, 39 confirmed, 0 falsified')
Code analysis reviewed: 64/67 WINs reviewed (verified from wins.json code_analysis tags)
Self-contradicted count: 12 WINs (verified from wins.json verdict field = 'Self-Contradicted')
